title: practical english usage - Part 246
content:
Could I have a look at the red ones?  I would prefer a small one.

Can I have one of those, please?    I'd like to see the wine list, please.

expressed as questions. Typical structures: Requests in shops, restaurants, etc are usually more direct, and are not always 3 shops,    restaurants,        etc grammar *311    politeness: distancing verb forms the children to school? This sounds like a complaint, ►    310.4.) but not You couldn't take the children to school, could you? (          Couldn't you take You wouldn't like to come out with us, by any chance?

I don't suppose you want to buy a car, do you?

If you would come this way...  I wonder if you could lend me £5?

What if we stayed at home for a change? Suppose I gave Alice a call?

or negative.

Another way of distancing suggestions from reality is to make them conditional 5 conditional        and   negative       expressions We would ask passengers to have their tickets ready for inspection.

kind of thing.

I'm surprised you didn't like the film. I would have thought it was just your I would think we might stop for lunch soon.

This is what I would call annoying.

I would say we'd do better to catch the earlier train.

a statement sound less definite.

Note the common use of would before verbs of saying and thinking, to make What would you like to drink?

and prefer.

Would is very often used to form requests and offers with verbs like like have been, exactly?' * I came in and ordered some shoes from you.' 'Oh  yes, sir. When would that I was wondering if you might be interested in a game of tennis.

We could ask Daniel to help us.

Could I ask you to translate this for me?

Could you give me a hand?

Hi! I thought I'd come over and introduce myself. My name's Andy.

I thought it would be nice to have a picnic.

suggestions less direct.

The  modal verbs would, could and might also make questions, requests and 4 modal     verbs:    would,       could      and   might Will you be going away this summer?

people's plans (►  41).

Future progressive verbs are often used to enquire politely about That will be £1.65,  please.

And will is sometimes used to say how  much money is owed.

I'll have to ask you to wait a minute.

have to can be used to soften instructions and orders.

Another way to distance something is to displace it into the future.  Will need / 3  future:    You'll     need      to   ...

I was thinking - what about borrowing Jake's car?

Were you looking for anything special? (in a shop) Good morning. I was wondering: do you need help moving that stuff?

Past progressives give two levels of distancing.
